perturbation.original_text: Any legal action arising out of or in connection with this Agreement or any breach hereof shall be brought and prosecuted in an appropriate court of competent jurisdiction in Santa Clara County or Los Angeles County, California.
perturbation.changed_text: Any legal action arising out of or in connection with this Agreement or any breach hereof shall be brought and prosecuted in a court of the Disclosing Party's choosing.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific county within California where legal actions must be initiated, the contract may violate California's Code of Civil Procedure regarding venue, especially if the Disclosing Party chooses a venue that is demonstrably inconvenient for the Receiving Party and lacks a sufficient connection to the transaction.
perturbation.law_explanation: CCP § 395 specifies venue rules for actions based on residence of the defendant or where the obligation is incurred. Allowing the Disclosing Party to choose any court, regardless of these rules, contradicts this law.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. OF THE PLACE OF TRIAL, RECLASSIFICATION, AND COORDINATION OF CIVIL ACTIONS [392 - 404.9] ( Heading of Title 4 amended by Stats. 1999, Ch. 344, Sec. 6. ) CHAPTER 1. Place of Trial [392 - 403] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1999, Ch. 344, Sec. 7. ) 395. (a) Except as otherwise provided by law and subject to the power of the court to transfer actions or proceedings as provided in this title, the superior court in the county where the defendants or some of them reside at the commencement of the action is the proper court for the trial of the action. If the action is for injury to person or personal property or for death from wrongful act or negligence, the superior court in either the county where the injury occurs or the injury causing death occurs or the county where the defendants, or some of them reside at the commencement of the action, is a proper court for the trial of the action. In a proceeding for dissolution of marriage, the superior court in the county where either the petitioner or respondent has been a resident for three months next preceding the commencement of the proceeding is the proper court for the trial of the proceeding. In a proceeding for nullit
perturbation.justification: The altered text, by allowing the Disclosing Party to choose any court, contradicts CCP § 395(a), which specifies that the proper venue is generally the county where the defendants reside. The original text limited venue to Santa Clara or Los Angeles County, aligning more closely with potential defendant residences or where the agreement was executed. The changed text undermines this by potentially forcing the Receiving Party to litigate in a distant or inconvenient location unrelated to the transaction or their residence, which is what CCP 395 seeks to prevent.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that litigation arises in connection with enforcement of any provision of this Agreement, the prevailing party in such litigation shall be entitled to recover its attorneys’ fees and expenses, in addition to any other relief to which it may be deemed entitled.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that litigation arises in connection with enforcement of any provision of this Agreement, each party shall bear its own attorneys’ fees and expenses.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that each party should bear its own legal fees, the contract seeks to prevent one party from collecting attorney's fees even when the other party is in breach. This conflicts with California Civil Code § 1717 regarding attorney's fees in contract actions.
perturbation.law_explanation: Cal. Civ. Code § 1717 states that in any action on a contract, where the contract specifically provides that attorney's fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce that contract, shall be awarded either to one of the parties or to the prevailing party, then the prevailing party shall be entitled to reasonable attorney's fees in addition to other costs.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1717 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1717. (a) In any action on a contract, where the contract specifically provides that attorney’s fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce that contract, shall be awarded either to one of the parties or to the prevailing party, then the party who is determined to be the party prevailing on the contract, whether he or she is the party specified in the contract or not, shall be entitled to reasonable attorney’s fees in addition to other costs. Where a contract provides for attorney’s fees, as set forth above, that provision shall be construed as applying to the entire contract, unless each party was represented by counsel in the negotiation and execution of the contract, and the fact of that representation is specified in the contract. Reasonable attorney’s fees shall be fixed by the court, and shall be an element of the costs of suit. Attorney’s fees provided for by this section shall not be subject to waiver by the parties to any contract which is entered into after the effective date of this section. Any provision in any such contract which provides for a waiver of attorney’s f
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts California Civil Code § 1717. The original text allows the prevailing party to recover attorney's fees, while the changed text states each party bears its own fees, which removes the possibility of fee recovery by the prevailing party. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, "In any action on a contract, where the contract specifically provides that attorney’s fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce that contract, shall be awarded either to one of the parties or to the prevailing party, then the party who is determined to be the party prevailing on the contract... shall be entitled to reasonable attorney’s fees in addition to other costs." The modified text eliminates this entitlement for the prevailing party, directly opposing the law's provision for awarding attorney's fees. This constitutes a significant contradiction, as it removes a right explicitly granted by statute.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement sets forth the entire understanding and agreement of the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes any and all prior or contemporaneous discussions, representations and understandings, whether written or oral.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement sets forth the entire understanding and agreement of the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'supersedes any and all prior or contemporaneous discussions, representations and understandings, whether written or oral,' the contract might allow for the admissibility of prior oral agreements, potentially conflicting with the California's parol evidence rule.
perturbation.law_explanation: CCP § 1856 is the parol evidence rule in California. While it has exceptions, generally it prevents extrinsic evidence of prior or contemporaneous agreements from contradicting or varying the terms of a completely integrated written contract. Removing the superseding clause weakens this protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1856 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 1. OF THE GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF EVIDENCE [1855 - 1866] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) 1856. (a) Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement. (b) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (c) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by course of dealing or usage of trade or by course of performance. (d) The court shall determine whether the writing is intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein and whether the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (e) Where a mistake or imperfection of the writing is put in issue by the pleadings, this section does not exclude evidence relevant to that issue. (f
perturbation.justification: The omission of the clause 'supersedes any and all prior or contemporaneous discussions, representations and understandings, whether written or oral' creates a legal contradiction because it potentially allows for the admissibility of prior oral agreements, conflicting with the parol evidence rule as codified in CCP § 1856(a). The original text explicitly prevented such admissibility, whereas the modified text does not, weakening the protection against extrinsic evidence contradicting the written agreement. CCP § 1856(a) states that terms set forth in a final written agreement 'may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement.' By removing the superseding clause, the contract now opens itself to potential contradictions from prior agreements, which the original text explicitly prevented.

perturbation.original_text: The formation, interpretation and performance of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: The formation, interpretation and performance of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of Nevada.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from California to Nevada could negatively impact Ixia, the California corporation in the contract. California has specific laws protecting businesses operating within the state. Nevada's business laws may not provide the same level of protection, contradicting California's interest in regulating business transactions within its borders. Note that choosing Nevada law may be permissible, but the change introduces legal risk of a legal challenge.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Corporations Code governs the formation, operation, and dissolution of corporations within California. By selecting Nevada law instead, the contract could potentially bypass certain mandatory provisions of California law designed to protect California corporations like IXIA, especially considering that the contract appears to be negotiated and executed in California, implying a sufficient nexus to California to warrant application of its laws.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Codes: Search skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search Code Search Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search California Constitution - CONS Business and Professions Code - BPC Civil Code - CIV Code of Civil Procedure - CCP Commercial Code - COM Corporations Code - CORP Education Code - EDC Elections Code - ELEC Evidence Code - EVID Family Code - FAM Financial Code - FIN Fish and Game Code - FGC Food and Agricultural Code - FAC Government Code - GOV Harbors and Navigation Code - HNC Health and Safety Code - HSC Insurance Code - INS Labor Code - LAB Military and Veterans Code - MVC Penal Code - PEN Probate Code - PROB Public Contract Code - PCC Public Resources Code - PRC Public Utilities Code - PUC Revenue and Taxation Code - RTC Streets and Highways Code - SHC Unemployment Insurance Code - UIC Vehicle Code - VEH Water Code - WAT Welfare and Institutions Code - WIC
perturbation.justification: The change from California law to Nevada law introduces a potential contradiction because it might circumvent California's regulations and protections for corporations operating within its borders, as stated in the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that California Corporations Code governs corporations within California. By choosing Nevada law, the contract risks bypassing mandatory provisions of California law designed to protect California corporations like IXIA, which is a California corporation.
